title: VMware Carbon Black Enterprise EDR Search Audit
spl:
index=audit sourcetype="carbonblack:edrsearch"
| stats count by query, analyst
| where count>50
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Splunk_Audit by _time span=1h | sort - count
description: EDR search auditing protects sensitive hunt activity from insider misuse.
value: EDR search auditing protects sensitive hunt activity from insider misuse.
grandmaExplanation: Search auditing protects sensitive hunt activity from insider misuse — so we know when protection is off, out of date, or bypassed.
implementation: Log heavy EDR federated searches. Review for data exfiltration via search export.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_vmware_carbonblack`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=carbonblack:edrsearch`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Log heavy EDR federated searches. Review for data exfiltration via search export.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=audit sourcetype="carbonblack:edrsearch"
| stats count by query, analyst
| where count>50
```

Understanding this SPL

**VMware Carbon Black Enterprise EDR Search Audit** — EDR search auditing protects sensitive hunt activity from insider misuse.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=carbonblack:edrsearch`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_vmware_carbonblack`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: audit; **sourcetype**: carbonblack:edrsearch. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Detection type** for this use case: TTP — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=audit, sourcetype="carbonblack:edrsearch". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by query, analyst** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Filters the current rows with `where count>50` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Splunk_Audit by _time span=1h | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**VMware Carbon Black Enterprise EDR Search Audit** — EDR search auditing protects sensitive hunt activity from insider misuse.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=carbonblack:edrsearch`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_vmware_carbonblack`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Detection type** for this use case: TTP — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Splunk_Audit` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (queries), Timeline.
knownFalsePositives: Legitimate use of dual-purpose administrative tools (e.g. PowerShell, SSH, cloud CLIs) during maintenance or deployment can match this pattern — correlate with change tickets, asset criticality and user role before escalating.
